input.abstract: We tested whether cognitive reappraisal, reframing a stressful situation in less threatening terms, reduces self-reported stress in healthy adults. In a randomized study, participants who practiced reappraisal over two weeks reported lower perceived stress and rumination than a control group. The technique is brief, requires no equipment, and can be self-administered in daily life.

Slow paced breathing at roughly six breaths per minute increased heart-rate variability and reduced state anxiety in a sample of undergraduates before an exam. Participants were guided through a five-minute paced-breathing exercise. Effects on subjective anxiety were measured immediately after. The protocol is simple enough to perform unaided.

A self-guided digital sleep-hygiene program delivered brief behavioral instructions (consistent wake time, limiting screens before bed, stimulus control). Adults with mild insomnia who followed the program for four weeks reported improved sleep onset latency and daytime focus relative to a waitlist. No therapist contact was required.

This randomized controlled trial compared two dosing schedules of sertraline in adults with major depressive disorder. Patients receiving the higher titration showed faster remission on the HAM-D but more gastrointestinal adverse events. Dosing was managed by a treating psychiatrist with weekly blood monitoring.

We examined the effect of a twelve-week plyometric and sprint-training block on sprint acceleration and vertical jump in elite collegiate sprinters. The intervention improved 30-meter sprint times and countermovement jump height. Findings are relevant to strength-and-conditioning coaches optimizing athletic performance.
